records.ety: [L. succedere, successum; sub under + cedere to go, to go along, approach, follow, succeed: cf. F. succder. See Cede, and cf. Success.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Succeeded; p. pr. & vb. n. Succeeding.]

defs: To follow in order; to come next after; hence, to take the place of; as, the king's eldest son succeeds his father on the throne; autumn succeeds summer.
quotes: As he saw him nigh succeed.

defs: To fall heir to; to inherit.
To come after; to be subsequent or consequent to; to follow; to pursue.
To support; to prosper; to promote.
quotes: Succeed my wish and second my design.

defs: To come in the place of another person, thing, or event; to come next in the usual, natural, or prescribed course of things; to follow; hence, to come next in the possession of anything; -- often with to.
quotes: If the father left only daughters, they equally succeeded to him in copartnership.
Enjoy till I return Short pleasures; for long woes are to succeed!

defs: To ascend the throne after the removal the death of the occupant.
quotes: No woman shall succeed in Salique land.

defs: To descend, as an estate or an heirloom, in the same family; to devolve.
To obtain the object desired; to accomplish what is attempted or intended; to have a prosperous issue or termination; to be successful; as, he succeeded in his plans; his plans succeeded.
quotes: It is almost impossible for poets to succeed without ambition.
Spenser endeavored it in Shepherd's Kalendar; but neither will it succeed in English.
Will you to the cooler cave succeed!
